content.text: Margaret Chen is a 68-year-old female presenting to the Emergency Department via EMS with progressive dyspnea on exertion over the past 5 days, now dyspneic at rest. She reports a 12-pound weight gain over the past 2 weeks, worsening bilateral lower extremity edema, and orthopnea requiring 4 pillows to sleep (baseline 2 pillows). She endorses paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea with 2-3 episodes per night over the last 3 nights. She admits to running out of furosemide 5 days ago and not refilling the prescription due to transportation difficulties (lives alone, no longer drives). She also reports decreased appetite, nausea, and fatigue limiting her ability to perform ADLs. She denies chest pain, syncope, or fever. In the ED, ECG revealed new-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (rate 128 bpm). BNP was critically elevated at 2,847 pg/mL. CXR showed bilateral pleural effusions and pulmonary vascular congestion. She was started on IV furosemide 80 mg and admitted to the cardiac step-down unit for acute decompensated heart failure management and rate control of new atrial fibrillation.

Widowed since 2020 (husband died of pancreatic cancer). Lives alone in a single-story ranch-style home. Retired elementary school teacher (30 years). One adult daughter (Linda, age 42) lives 45 minutes away, works full-time as an accountant, visits weekends. Two grandchildren (ages 8 and 11). Former social drinker, quit alcohol 2020. Never smoker. No illicit drugs. Relies on medical transport for appointments – scheduling difficulties. Church community provides some social support. Reports feeling isolated and lonely since husband's death. Has a cat (Mimi). Diet: mostly canned soups and frozen meals due to fatigue and limited motivation to cook. Admits to high sodium intake. Financial: fixed income (teacher pension + Social Security), reports difficulty affording some medications.

Prior to admission: independent with bathing/dressing but takes longer due to dyspnea and knee/hip pain. Uses cane for ambulation >50 feet. Manages stairs (5 steps to front door) with railing but avoids them. Drives rarely – license valid but avoids due to anxiety. Manages own medications with weekly pill organizer but admits occasional missed doses. Prepares simple meals. Daughter helps with grocery shopping and heavy cleaning.
Alert, oriented x4, appears fatigued, mild respiratory distress, speaking in short sentences. Sitting upright at 45 degrees.
Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes dry. JVD present at 10 cm above sternal angle at 45 degrees.
Irregular rhythm, rate ~128 bpm. S1/S2 with S3 gallop. No S4 or murmur. PMI displaced laterally to 6th ICS, anterior axillary line. No carotid bruits.

Bilateral basilar crackles extending to mid-lung fields. Decreased breath sounds at bases bilaterally. Dullness to percussion at bilateral bases consistent with effusions. RR 24, mild accessory muscle use.
Soft, mildly distended, non-tender. Hepatomegaly with liver edge 3 cm below right costal margin. Positive hepatojugular reflux. Bowel sounds present.
3+ pitting edema bilateral LE extending to mid-thigh. Skin taut and shiny over shins. Bilateral varicosities. Pedal pulses 1+ bilaterally. Capillary refill 4 seconds in toes.
Alert and oriented x4. CN II-XII intact. Strength 4/5 bilateral LE (limited by edema/fatigue), 5/5 UE. Sensation intact.
Intact. Erythema bilateral lower shins from edema. Dry skin bilateral feet with mild heel cracking. No pressure injuries. Braden Score: 15 (mild risk).
Flat affect, tearful at times. Reports feeling 'overwhelmed' and 'like a burden.' PHQ-9: 14 (moderate depression). Denies SI/HI.

rows: Anaphylaxis (2003) – throat swelling, hypotension, required epinephrine
HFrEF diagnosed 2022, last EF 35% (echo 2025-09), NYHA Class II at baseline
NEW onset – identified on admission ECG; no prior history of arrhythmia
Diagnosed 2008, oral agents + basal insulin since 2021, HbA1c 8.2% (2026-01)
Diagnosed 2005, previously controlled on triple therapy
BMI 34.0, weight trending up 8 lbs over past 6 months
Diagnosed 2020 after husband's death, on sertraline, PHQ-9=14 today
Bilateral knees, left hip; limits mobility; uses cane at home
CHF – missed doses x 5 days per pt report

Cardiomegaly CTR 0.62. Bilateral pleural effusions moderate R, small-moderate L. Perihilar congestion with cephalization. Bilateral interstitial edema, Kerley B lines at bases. No consolidation or pneumothorax.
1. Cardiomegaly. 2. Bilateral pleural effusions (R>L). 3. Pulmonary congestion consistent with acute decompensated HF. 4. No pneumonia.

LV severely dilated (LVEDD 6.2 cm), global hypokinesis. EF 30% by Simpson's biplane. Grade II diastolic dysfunction. E/A 1.2, E/e' 18. RV mildly dilated, TAPSE 1.6 cm. LA moderately dilated (vol index 42 mL/m²). Moderate MR (vena contracta 5 mm). Moderate TR. RVSP 48 mmHg. Aortic valve trileaflet, no stenosis, trace AR. No pericardial effusion. IVC dilated 2.4 cm, <50% collapse.

1. Severely reduced LVEF (30%). 2. Grade II diastolic dysfunction. 3. Moderate MR. 4. Moderate TR, elevated RVSP (48 mmHg). 5. Dilated LA. 6. Dilated IVC (elevated RA pressure). 7. No pericardial effusion.
Cardiomegaly stable. Bilateral pleural effusions significantly decreased – now small bilateral. Congestion improved, cephalization resolved. Mild residual interstitial edema. No new infiltrate.
1. Improving bilateral pleural effusions. 2. Improving congestion. 3. Stable cardiomegaly. 4. No superimposed infection.

Bilateral CFV, SFV, popliteal, and calf veins evaluated. Normal compressibility and flow augmentation. No intraluminal thrombus. Bilateral saphenous veins patent. Subcutaneous edema noted bilaterally.
1. No DVT bilateral LE. 2. Bilateral subcutaneous edema consistent with clinical history.
HFrEF – reduced dose for renal function
HFrEF – reduced dose for renal function
HFrEF – reduced dose for renal function
HFrEF – reduced dose for renal function
Admit to 4-South Cardiac Step-down, Attending: Dr. Rivera
Bedrest with BRP x 24 hours, then advance as tolerated
2g Sodium-restricted, Diabetic/Cardiac diet, 1500 mL fluid restriction
Daily weights – before breakfast, same scale
Vital signs q4h with pulse oximetry
Neuro checks q4h x 24 hrs (new A-fib)
BMP, CBC q AM; BNP daily; Troponin q8h x 3
Metoprolol 5 mg IV q6h for HR >110
Heparin infusion per protocol – target aPTT 60-80 sec
KCl 40 mEq PO BID – maintain K+ >4.0
Hold home carvedilol – replace with metoprolol
Hold home lisinopril – restart when Cr stabilizes
Insulin glargine 22 units SubQ QHS, lispro SSI AC+HS
Fall precautions – Morse Fall Scale assessment q shift
Skin assessment q shift – Braden scale monitoring
Elevate HOB 30-45 degrees at all times
Apply SCDs bilateral lower extremities when in bed
Start Warfarin 5 mg PO daily at 1700
Restart Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily (reduced dose)
Physical Therapy – mobility assessment and cardiac rehab eval
Social Work – discharge planning, home safety, resources
Up to chair with assist TID, ambulate 50 feet BID
Pharmacy – medication reconciliation and warfarin education
Reduce furosemide to 60 mg IV q12h
D/C Heparin infusion – transition to warfarin
Start Metoprolol succinate 25 mg PO daily
Ambulate 100 feet TID with standby assist
D/C IV furosemide – Furosemide 80 mg PO BID
Increase metoprolol succinate to 50 mg daily
Adjust warfarin to 4 mg PO daily (INR 1.8)
Ambulate 200 feet QID, stair training with PT
Discharge education: daily weights, fluid restriction, low-Na diet, meds, when to call MD
Arrange medical transport for follow-up appointments

68F admitted with acute decompensated HFrEF (EF 30% on bedside echo) and new-onset A-fib with RVR. Etiology: medication non-adherence (missed furosemide x 5 days) and dietary indiscretion (high sodium). BNP 2847. CXR bilateral effusions. Started aggressive IV diuresis and rate control. Holding metformin for AKI on CKD. CHA2DS2-VASc=5. Plan: IV furosemide, heparin drip, metoprolol IV, echo in AM, EP consult, SW for discharge planning.

Patient arrived from ED via stretcher, alert and oriented x4 but exhausted. Telemetry A-fib RVR rate 128. O2 sat 89% RA, placed on 4L NC improving to 93%. JVD, bilateral crackles, 3+ LE edema. Foley placed for strict I&O. IV: 20G right AC. Morse 65 (high risk). Braden 15 (mild risk). Patient tearful, states she feels like a burden. Oriented to room and telemetry. Call light within reach, bed low, non-skid socks.

Patient resting semi-Fowler's. 800 mL output since admission. HR 118 A-fib. BP 148/86. O2 91% on 3L NC. Bilateral crackles persistent. Edema 3+. Oriented x4, difficulty sleeping (orthopnea, frequent urination). HS glucose 242 – gave 4 units lispro SSI. Pain 4/10 bilateral knees, acetaminophen 650 mg given, relief to 2/10.

Day 1. Net negative 2.1L overnight. Weight 195.2 (-3.2 lbs). Cr 1.9 (from 2.1) – tolerating diuresis. BNP 1920. HR 118, A-fib improving with metoprolol. O2 decreased to 3L NC. Plan: continue IV diuresis, add digoxin, start warfarin, consult PT/SW/RD. Restart lisinopril at reduced dose.

Weight 195.2 lbs (-3.2). I&O: intake 1200/output 3300 in 24h. Sat in chair 30 min with assist of 1, tolerated but fatigued and mildly dizzy on standing. HR 114 irregular. BP 150/86. O2 92% 3L NC. Crackles bases improved from mid-lung. Edema 3+ but patient says legs 'a little less tight.' Fasting BG 186 – correction dose given. Patient anxious about finances and managing at home.

Cardiac rehab/mobility eval. Deconditioned with baseline OA. Bed mobility: moderate assist. Transfers: moderate assist. Ambulated 50 ft x2 with rolling walker and moderate assist – HR 112→132, O2 92%→88%, required 2-min rest. Morse 65. Recommend: progressive mobility, assistive device eval, cardiac rehab referral. Goal: independent ambulation 200+ ft with walker by discharge.

Met with patient, daughter Linda by phone. Lives alone, single-story (5 exterior steps with railing). Barriers: transportation (unreliable), medication access (cost + transport), social isolation, depression (PHQ-9:14), limited cooking. Daughter works full-time, limited M-F. Church community willing but uncoordinated. Discussed home health, Meals on Wheels, medication delivery, Area Agency on Aging. Patient receptive but tearful.

Obese (BMI 34), albumin 2.9, prealbumin 12.4 (malnourished despite obesity). High Na intake (canned soups, frozen meals, est 4-6g/day). HbA1c 8.2%. CKD 3b. Recs: 2g Na restriction, 1500 mL fluid restriction, consistent carb diet (45-60g/meal), protein 0.8 g/kg/day. Educated on label reading and low-Na alternatives. Recommend Meals on Wheels for low-Na meals.

Day 2. Weight 191.8 (-6.6 total). Cr 1.8 stable. BNP 1204. HR 104 A-fib. O2 weaned to 2L NC. Crackles bases only. Edema 2+. D/C heparin, INR 1.3, continue warfarin. Transition to PO metoprolol. Reduce furosemide 60 mg IV q12h. Patient more alert. Plan: continue diuresis, formal echo today, dig level tomorrow.

Weight 191.8. Ambulated 80 ft x2 with walker and standby assist. HR 116 during ambulation, recovered within 2 min. O2 >92% on 2L NC. Fluid restriction – patient struggling with thirst; provided strategies (ice chips, candy, mouth care). Edema 2+ bilateral LE. Skin intact, SCDs continued. Foley d/c'd – voiding adequately. BG 154 fasting. Patient slightly more engaged. Fall precautions maintained.

Key issues: (1) Metformin held for AKI – reassess Cr<1.5. (2) Warfarin interactions – monitor INR with atorvastatin. (3) Digoxin dose appropriate for renal function – monitor levels. (4) K+ adequate but monitor with ACEi. (5) Aspirin held on heparin/warfarin. (6) Insulin SSI appropriate. (7) Discharge: 90-day supply, recommend pharmacy delivery. Education: warfarin diet interactions, bleeding signs.

Day 3. Weight 188.6 (-9.8 total). Cr 1.7. BNP 680. HR 88, converting to sinus intermittently. Echo formal: EF 30%, moderate MR, moderate TR, dilated LA, Grade II diastolic dysfunction. Transition PO furosemide 80 mg BID. Uptitrate metoprolol 50 mg. CXR: effusions decreased, mild residual congestion. INR 1.8 – adjust warfarin 4 mg. Start enoxaparin bridge. LE duplex ordered for persistent edema.

Significant improvement. Ambulated 150 ft x2 with walker and standby assist. HR 78-94, O2 >94% on 1L NC. Stair training: 4 steps up/down with bilateral rail and standby assist. More confident but still needs assistive device. Recommend home PT 2x/week and outpatient cardiac rehab.

Weight 188.6. O2 weaned to 1L NC, sat 95%. HR 88, sinus with intermittent A-fib. Edema 1-2+ bilateral LE. Crackles faint bilateral bases. Ambulating to bathroom independently with walker. Appetite improving – ate 75% meals. BG 132 fasting. Warfarin education with pharmacist completed. Patient nervous about going home alone. Skin intact, Braden 18.

Planning conference with patient, daughter (phone), PT, RD, RN, case manager. Plan: (1) Home health RN 3x/week, PT 2x/week. (2) Meals on Wheels 5 days/week low-Na. (3) Medical transport for f/u. (4) Pharmacy delivery via Walgreens. (5) LifeAlert pendant. (6) Area Agency on Aging: 4 hrs/week home aide. (7) Outpatient cardiac rehab. (8) F/u: Cardiology 1 wk, PCP 2 wk, Endocrinology 3 wk. Daughter will come for discharge, stay first 2 nights.

Day 4. Ready for discharge. Weight 186.4 (-12.0 lbs). Cr 1.6 (baseline). BNP 342. HR 78 predominantly sinus. INR 2.2 therapeutic. O2 96% RA. Lungs clear. Edema 1+ ankles. Ambulating 200+ ft with walker. Discharge with: Furosemide 80 mg BID, Metoprolol succinate 50 mg, Digoxin 0.125 mg, Warfarin 4 mg (INR 2-3), Lisinopril 10 mg, Insulin glargine 22u QHS, Glipizide 10 mg, Sertraline 100 mg, Pantoprazole 40 mg, Levothyroxine 75 mcg, Atorvastatin 40 mg, KCl 40 mEq BID, Acetaminophen PRN. Hold: metformin, aspirin, amlodipine, carvedilol.

VS stable: HR 78 sinus, BP 130/76, O2 96% RA. Weight 186.4. Daughter present. Reviewed all discharge instructions with teach-back: daily weight (call MD >2 lbs/day or >5 lbs/week), 1500 mL fluid restriction, 2g Na diet, medication schedule. Warfarin education reinforced. Fall precautions: walker, non-skid shoes, clear paths, nightlights. LifeAlert activated. Follow-up confirmed. Home health Day 2 post-discharge. Prescriptions to Walgreens with delivery. Discharged via wheelchair at 1130.

Final education with patient and daughter. Reviewed 2g Na diet with meal plans/recipes. Low-Na grocery list and dining-out guide. Carb counting for DM. Fluid restriction strategies. Patient can identify high-Na foods and describe portion control. Daughter committed to weekend grocery/meal prep. Meals on Wheels starts Day 2 post-discharge.

Reviewed 14 discharge meds with patient and daughter. Key: (1) Warfarin: same time daily, no doubles, consistent vitamin K, avoid NSAIDs, report bleeding. (2) Furosemide: AM + early afternoon, weigh daily. (3) Digoxin: check pulse, hold if <60. (4) Insulin: storage, technique. (5) Organizer set up. (6) Wallet med card provided. Teach-back verified.

content.text: Patient: Margaret Chen  |  MRN: MCH-2026-044871  |  DOB: 1957-08-14
Attending: Dr. James Rivera, MD (Cardiology)  |  PCP: Dr. Susan Ngai, MD (Internal Medicine)
Acute decompensated heart failure (HFrEF, EF 30%) with new-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.

Margaret Chen is a 68-year-old female presenting to the Emergency Department via EMS with progressive dyspnea on exertion over the past 5 days, now dyspneic at rest. She reports a 12-pound weight gain over the past 2 weeks, worsening bilateral lower extremity edema, and orthopnea requiring 4 pillows to sleep (baseline 2 pillows). She endorses paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea with 2-3 episodes per night over the last 3 nights. She admits to running out of furosemide 5 days ago and not refilling the prescription due to transportation difficulties (lives alone, no longer drives). She also reports decreased appetite, nausea, and fatigue limiting her ability to perform ADLs. She denies chest pain, syncope, or fever. In the ED, ECG revealed new-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (rate 128 bpm). BNP was critically elevated at 2,847 pg/mL. CXR showed bilateral pleural effusions and pulmonary vascular congestion. She was started on IV furosemide 80 mg and admitted to the cardiac step-down unit for acute decompensated heart failure management and rate control of new atrial fibrillation.
HF (HFrEF, EF 30%, NYHA II baseline), NEW A-fib, T2DM (HbA1c 8.2%), HTN, CKD 3b (Cr 1.6-1.8), Obesity (BMI 34), MDD (PHQ-9=14), OA (bilateral knees, left hip), GERD, Hypothyroidism.

68-year-old widow living alone. Retired teacher. Daughter Linda (42) 45 min away. Limited transport. Fixed income. Social isolation and depression since husband's death (2020). High-Na diet. Missed furosemide x 5 days precipitating admission.

5-day stay: aggressive IV diuresis (12.0-lb loss), A-fib rate control (HR 128→78), anticoagulation (heparin→warfarin, INR 2.2), glycemic management, progressive mobilization (bedrest→200+ ft with walker), comprehensive discharge planning with home health, Meals on Wheels, community services.

Priority #1: Excess Fluid Volume r/t compromised cardiac pump function and medication non-adherence AEB 12-lb weight gain, 3+ bilateral LE edema, bilateral pleural effusions, JVD, S3 gallop, BNP 2847 pg/mL, pulmonary crackles to mid-lung fields
- Weight: 198.4 lbs admit (12-lb gain over 2 weeks); dry weight ~186 lbs
- 3+ pitting edema bilateral LE to mid-thigh
- Bilateral crackles to mid-lung; bilateral pleural effusions on CXR
- JVD 10 cm above sternal angle; hepatojugular reflux; S3 gallop
- Na 131 (dilutional hyponatremia); missed furosemide x 5 days
Day 1: Wt 195.2 (-3.2), net -2.1L, BNP 1920. Crackles to bases. Edema 3+. → Partial.
Day 2: Wt 191.8 (-6.6), net -1.8L, BNP 1204. Edema 2+. → Meeting goals.
Day 3: Wt 188.6 (-9.8), BNP 680. CXR improved. Edema 1-2+. → Significant progress.
Day 4: Wt 186.4 (-12.0 near dry wt), BNP 342. Lungs clear. Edema 1+. RA. → Goals met.
Priority #2: Decreased Cardiac Output r/t altered heart rate/rhythm (new A-fib) and reduced contractility (EF 30%) AEB tachycardia HR 128, irregular rhythm, fatigue, dyspnea, S3 gallop, EF 30%
NANDA-I Domain: Activity/Rest  |  Class: CV/Pulmonary Responses
- New-onset A-fib RVR: HR 128 on admission
- Echo: EF 30%, global hypokinesis, LVEDD 6.2 cm
- Moderate MR, moderate TR, RVSP 48 mmHg
- Capillary refill 4 sec toes; pedal pulses 1+
Day 1: HR 118 A-fib. Chair 30 min. aPTT 72 (therapeutic). → Partial.
Day 2: HR 104. PO metoprolol. INR 1.3. Ambulated 80 ft. → Improving.
Day 3: HR 88, intermittent sinus. INR 1.8. Dig 0.8. → Good progress.
Day 4: HR 78 sinus. INR 2.2 (therapeutic). Demonstrates pulse check. → Met.
Priority #3: Impaired Gas Exchange r/t V/Q mismatch from pulmonary congestion and pleural effusions AEB SpO2 89% on RA, RR 24, bilateral crackles, accessory muscle use, dyspnea at rest
NANDA-I Domain: Elimination/Exchange  |  Class: Respiratory Function
- SpO2 89% RA admission; required 4L NC for >92%
- Bilateral crackles to mid-lung; CXR bilateral effusions, Kerley B lines
Day 1: SpO2 91-93% 3L NC. RR 20-22. Crackles bases. PND less frequent. → Partial.
Day 3: SpO2 94-95% 1L NC. RR 16-18. CXR improved. → Approaching goal.
Day 4: SpO2 96% RA. RR 16. Lungs clear. No dyspnea at rest. → Met.

Priority #4: Activity Intolerance r/t O2 supply/demand imbalance from reduced CO and deconditioning AEB exertional dyspnea, HR increase to 132 with ambulation, SpO2 drop to 88%, moderate assist needed
- PT eval: moderate assist bed mobility and transfers
- Morse 65 (high risk); baseline cane for >50 ft
- NYHA II baseline, presenting Class IV
Day 1: Chair 30 min mod assist. 50 ft x2. HR 132, SpO2 88%. → Working toward goals.
Day 2: 80 ft x2 standby assist. HR 116, SpO2 >92%. → Improving.
Day 3: 150 ft x2. Stairs 4 steps. HR 94. → Significant progress.
Day 4: 200+ ft independent with walker. Stairs with rail. HR 86. → Met.
Priority #5: Unstable Blood Glucose r/t acute illness, med changes (metformin held), dietary inconsistency AEB glucose 218 mg/dL, HbA1c 8.2%, BG range 118-242
- Home: Metformin 1000 BID + Glipizide 10 + Glargine 22u QHS
- Metformin held for AKI (Cr 2.1) – lactic acidosis risk
- High carb intake at home; CKD limits med options
Day 1: BG 186-242. 2 corrections. Carb diet started. → Partial.
Day 2: BG 154-198. 1 correction. Engaged with RD. → Improving.
Day 3: BG 132-168. Within target. Practicing carb counting. → Met.
Day 4: BG 118 fasting, range 118-142. Demonstrates monitoring. → Met.
Priority #6: Risk for Impaired Skin Integrity r/t peripheral edema, immobility, malnutrition (albumin 2.8, prealbumin 12.4), diabetes, moisture exposure
NANDA-I Domain: Safety/Protection  |  Class: Physical Injury
- 3+ edema bilateral LE – skin taut, shiny, fragile
- Dry feet with heel cracking; erythema bilateral shins
Day 2: Skin intact. Edema 2+. Braden 16. Heel cracking treated. → No breakdown.
Day 3: Skin intact. Edema 1-2+. Braden 17. → Met.
Day 4: Skin intact. Edema 1+. Braden 18. No pressure injuries. → All met.

Priority #7: Deficient Knowledge r/t complex medication regimen, dietary modifications, self-monitoring, disease management AEB medication non-adherence, high-Na diet, weight gain, lack of symptom recognition
- Ran out of furosemide 5 days prior (transport + cost barriers)
- High Na intake (canned soups, frozen meals) est 4-6g/day
- Did not recognize worsening edema/dyspnea as HF exacerbation
- 15 home meds; new additions: warfarin, digoxin, metoprolol
- New self-monitoring: daily weights, pulse check, INR, BG
- Retired teacher – good cognitive baseline, oriented x4
- Barriers: lives alone, limited transport, fixed income, depression
Day 2: Engaged in diet education. 3 high-Na foods identified. Fluid concept difficult. → In progress.
Day 3: Warfarin education done. Pulse check demonstrated. Weight parameters verbalized. → Good progress.
Day 4: Comprehensive teach-back with daughter. All meds correct. Techniques demonstrated. → All met.

Priority #8: Risk for Loneliness/Social Isolation r/t loss of spouse, living alone, limited transportation, depression (PHQ-9=14), reduced functional capacity AEB statements of feeling 'overwhelmed' and 'like a burden,' flat affect, tearfulness
- Widowed since 2020; primary social companion lost
- Lives alone; daughter visits weekends only
- Reports isolation, loneliness, feeling 'like a burden'
- Reduced functional capacity limits social activities
- Fixed income with med affordability concerns
Day 1: SW consulted. Patient tearful, fears living alone. Receptive to services. → Initiated.
Day 3: Identified daughter, church friends, MOW driver as support. Agreed to services. → Progress.
Day 4: Supports in place. States 'less scared.' Daughter present. LifeAlert active. → Met for DC.
Comparison of home medications to discharge medications with clinical rationale.
Total discharge medications: 17 (including PRN). Medication list card provided. Pharmacy delivery arranged.

Morse Fall Scale: 65 admission (high risk ≥45). Factors: secondary diagnosis (+15), ambulatory aid (+30), IV therapy (+20), gait impaired (+20), oriented (0). Improved to 45 at discharge (moderate) as IV d/c'd and mobility improved.

Bed lowest position. Call light within reach. Non-skid socks. Room clear of clutter. Bed alarm when unattended. Fall risk armband. Toileting q2h. Assist with ambulation per order. Walker within reach. Orthostatic VS with position changes. Meds reviewed for fall risk (beta-blocker, digoxin).

Rolling walker for all ambulation. Non-skid footwear. Remove throw rugs and clutter. Nightlights in bedroom/hallway/bathroom. Grab bars in bathroom. LifeAlert pendant – wear at all times. Home PT 2x/week. Rise slowly (orthostatic precautions). Report dizziness to MD.

1. Cardiology (Dr. Rivera): 1 week – weight, INR, meds
2. PCP (Dr. Ngai): 2 weeks – comprehensive review, renal function, consider metformin
3. Endocrinology: 3 weeks – diabetes optimization
4. INR clinic: 2-3 days post-DC, then per protocol
5. Cardiac rehab: start 2 weeks post-DC
Valley Home Health Services. RN 3x/week x 4 weeks: meds, VS, weights, INR draws, HF education. PT 2x/week x 4 weeks: mobility, balance, cardiac exercises, home safety. Start Day 2 post-DC.

1. Meals on Wheels: low-Na M-F, starting Day 2
2. Medical transport: all follow-ups
3. Pharmacy delivery: Walgreens
4. Area Agency on Aging: home aide 4 hrs/week
5. LifeAlert pendant activated
6. Church volunteers coordinated by pastor

Daughter Linda present, stays first 2 nights. Prescriptions to Walgreens with delivery. Equipment: rolling walker, LifeAlert. Discharged via wheelchair at 1130. Transport scheduled for cardiology 1-week f/u.

PHQ-9=14 (moderate). Sertraline 100 mg continued. Reports feeling 'overwhelmed' and 'like a burden.' Flat affect, tearful. Denies SI. Plan: continue sertraline, PCP reassess 2 weeks (consider dose adjustment/therapy), home health RN screen PHQ-2 each visit, cardiac rehab for social/physical benefits, community mental health if PHQ-9 remains ≥10.

Risk factors: widowed, alone, limited transport, reduced function, depression. Services: home health RN 3x/week, Meals on Wheels 5x/week (welfare check), home aide 4 hrs/week, church volunteers, cardiac rehab group, daily phone/video with daughter and grandchildren, LifeAlert for safety.

Primary: daughter Linda (42, accountant, 45 min away, full-time M-F). Educated on HF management, meds, warning signs. Connected with Caregiver Support Group via Area Agency on Aging. Weekend responsibilities: groceries, meal prep, organizer refill. Respite resources provided.

Faith community important. Pastor at First Methodist notified, committed to visits and volunteer coordination. Resume church attendance via transport when able (cardiac rehab goal). Grief process (husband 2020) acknowledged as ongoing factor.
Prepared By: RN S. Patel, BSN, RN  |  Date: 2026-03-14
Reviewed By: Dr. James Rivera, MD (Cardiology)  |  Date: 2026-03-14
Developed collaboratively with cardiology, nursing, PT, SW, RD, and clinical pharmacy. Patient and family active participants.

rows: Verbalize daily weight monitoring and fluid restriction understanding
Administer IV furosemide as ordered (80→60→PO transition)
ACC/AHA 2022: loop diuresis first-line for ADHF
Strict I&O q shift; report UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr
Guides diuretic dosing and renal perfusion assessment
Daily weights before breakfast, same scale; report gain >2 lbs/day
Most reliable fluid status indicator in HF
Enforce 1500 mL/day fluid restriction; ice chips, mouth care, candy
Reduces preload and prevents further volume overload
Na restriction reduces fluid retention; HF pts limit <2g/day
Monitor BMP daily: Na, K, Cr, BUN
Diuresis causes electrolyte depletion and renal changes
Tracks decongestion effectiveness and guides discharge readiness
Elevate HOB 30-45°; elevate LE when sitting
Apply SCDs; assess skin under edema q shift
DVT prophylaxis; edematous skin fragile and prone to breakdown
Auscultate lungs q4h; monitor RR and SpO2
Tolerate chair 30 min without hemodynamic compromise
Continuous telemetry; report HR >120 or <50, new arrhythmia, ST changes
Detects rate/rhythm changes in new A-fib
Rate-control meds: Metoprolol IV→PO, Digoxin 0.125 mg
INR daily (target 2.0-3.0); adjust warfarin per protocol
Prevents stroke while minimizing bleeding risk
Neuro checks q4h; assess peripheral pulses, skin color
A-fib increases stroke risk 5-fold; early detection critical
Assess bleeding: bruising, hematuria, melena, gum bleeding
Digoxin level Day 3; hold if HR <60 or toxicity signs
Narrow therapeutic index; CKD increases toxicity risk
Prevents deconditioning while monitoring cardiac response
Teach pulse self-check; hold digoxin if <60
Patient safety with digoxin depends on pulse monitoring
No dyspnea at rest; ambulate without significant SOB
Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 ≥92%; titrate down
Improves diaphragmatic excursion and reduces congestion
Auscultate lungs q4h; document crackle progression
Tracks pulmonary edema resolution with diuresis
Monitor RR, depth, pattern, WOB q4h
IS q2h while awake (goal 1000 mL); deep breathing
Resolving pulmonary edema improves gas exchange
Monitor for respiratory distress: cyanosis, AMS
100 ft with standby assist, HR increase <20%
Safe walker use and stair navigation
Monitor HR, BP, SpO2, symptoms before/during/after activity
Stop if: HR >130/<50, SBP drop >20, SpO2 <90, CP, severe dyspnea
Daily PT: progressive distance, walker training, stairs
Expert-guided safe progression and device selection
Energy conservation education: sit for tasks, pace activities
Self-management strategies for independent home function
Pain assessment before activity; acetaminophen 30 min prior PRN
Verbalize carb counting and BG monitoring
BG AC and HS (4x daily); report >250 or <70
Guides insulin dosing in acute illness
